# ARIA FinOps + ACES Platform — Complete Workspace Bible **Status:** Production-Ready | **Stack:** Oracle 23c · Spring AI · React 18 · Java 21 This document contains the consolidated technical output, architectural designs, and initialization instructions generated during our session. You can copy this directly into your GitHub repository as a master documentation file (e.g., `PROJECT_BIBLE.md`) or use it to scaffold your project. --- ## 🚀 PART 1: AI Workspace Initialization Prompt **Instruction:** Copy the prompt below and paste it into your AI assistant (Copilot/Claude) to generate the full project structure. ```markdown # ARIA WORKSPACE INITIALIZATION PROMPT You are an expert enterprise architect and senior engineer helping me set up the ARIA FinOps + ACES platform workspace. ## Context I am building ARIA (Adaptive Risk Intelligence for AI Agents) — a production-grade governance and cost intelligence platform with these constraints: - Stack: Java 21, Spring Boot 3.3, Spring AI 1.0, Oracle 23c Autonomous DB, React 18, TypeScript - Architecture: SDD (Spec-Driven Development), plugin-based, Oracle VPD for isolation - Key modules: Cost Intelligence Engine, Trust-Cost Feedback Loop, Executive FinOps Dashboard, ACES MCP execution - Compliance: SOX/DORA audit trail, 7-year retention, PII-safe, developer-access only (no DBA privileges) ## Your Task Help me initialize my workspace by generating the following artifacts IN ORDER. Wait for my confirmation after each step before proceeding. ### STEP 1: Project Structure & Root Files Generate: 1. `justfile` — orchestration pipeline with targets: validate-env, generate-code, build-artifacts, deploy-pilot, validate-e2e, demo 2. `.env.example` — environment template with Oracle wallet path, Supabase ref, K8s namespace, signing key placeholders 3. `README.md` — project overview, quick start, architecture diagram reference 4. Directory structure: ``` aria-workspace/ ├── specs/ # SDD contracts ├── src/ # React frontend + shared libs ├── backend/ # Spring Boot modules ├── k8s/ # Kubernetes manifests ├── supabase/ # Migrations + RLS policies ├── oracle/ # DDL scripts (developer-access safe) ├── plugins/ # IDE plugin sources ├── scripts/ # Bash utility scripts └── docs/ # Architecture, runbooks, compliance ``` ### STEP 2: SDD Spec Contracts Generate these JSON/YAML specs with strict validation schemas: 1. `specs/skill-manifest.spec.json` — FinOps skill contract (input/output, configSchema, permissions) 2. `specs/ide-interceptor.contract.yaml` — OpenAPI 3.1 spec for IDE ↔ ARIA API 3. `specs/trust-cost-feedback.schema.json` — JSON Schema for cost → trust score mapping ### STEP 3: Oracle 23c Schema (Developer Access) Generate `oracle/01_finops_schema.sql` with: - `aria_model_pricing` table (dynamic pricing catalog) - `aria_cost_ledger` table (partitioned by month, virtual cost columns) - `aria_agent_dna` extensions (cost_efficiency_score, total_cost_usd, budget fields) - Indexes for executive queries (LOB, agent, model, timestamp) - Seed data for Anthropic/OpenAI model pricing - NO DBMS_RLS/VPD calls — app-layer isolation only ### STEP 4: Spring Boot Module Scaffold Generate `backend/aria-finops-api/build.gradle.kts` and package structure: ``` com.jpmc.aria.finops/ ├── advisor/ │ └── AriaCostInterceptor.java (ChatClientAdvisor, <8ms overhead) ├── model/ │ ├── CostTransaction.java │ ├── CostMetrics.java │ └── BudgetStatus.java ├── repository/ │ ├── CostLedgerRepository.java │ └── AgentDnaRepository.java ── service/ │ ├── PricingCatalog.java │ ├── BudgetGovernor.java │ └── OptimizationEngine.java └── config/ └── OracleConfig.java (ADB wallet connection, no DBA privileges) ``` ### STEP 5: React Dashboard Component Generate `src/pages/ExecutiveFinOps.tsx` with: - 4 KPI cards (Total Cost, Savings Realized, Governance Impact, Anomalies) - LOB breakdown chart (Digital Banking, Risk, Payments, Trading) - Optimization impact table (Model Downgrade, Compression, Cache, Pruning) - Governance decision grid (BLOCK/REVIEW/ALLOW) - Tailwind CSS styling, Lucide React icons, TypeScript types ### STEP 6: Validation Scripts Generate `scripts/00-validate-env.sh` that checks: - Oracle wallet connectivity (developer credentials only) - Supabase project access - Kubectl context + namespace - Node/Java toolchain - SDD spec presence ## Constraints - All code must be production-ready: type-safe, error-handled, logged - Never include hardcoded secrets — use environment variables or secret managers - Oracle DDL must work with developer-level privileges only (no DBMS_RLS, no ALTER SYSTEM) - Latency: CostInterceptor pre-check ≤8ms, async post-processing only - Security: App-layer LOB isolation via JWT claims + repository filters ## Output Format For each step: 1. Show the file path 2. Provide the complete file content in a code block 3. Add brief comments explaining critical sections 4. Wait for my "✅ Step N complete" before proceeding to next step Begin with STEP 1 now. ``` --- ## 🗄️ PART 2: Oracle 23c Schema (Developer Access) **File:** `oracle/01_finops_schema.sql` *Note: No DBA privileges required. Uses standard DDL and developer-friendly constraints.* ```sql -- ============================================ -- ARIA FINOPS SCHEMA — ORACLE 23C -- Developer Access Compatible (No DBA) -- ============================================ -- 1. Model Pricing Catalog (Dynamic, versioned) CREATE TABLE aria_model_pricing ( pricing_id NUMBER GENERATED BY DEFAULT AS IDENTITY PRIMARY KEY, provider VARCHAR2(50) NOT NULL, model_name VARCHAR2(100) NOT NULL, model_version VARCHAR2(50), input_price_per_1k NUMBER(10,6) NOT NULL, output_price_per_1k NUMBER(10,6) NOT NULL, cached_price_per_1k NUMBER(10,6), max_context_tokens INTEGER NOT NULL, capabilities JSON, is_active CHAR(1) DEFAULT 'Y' CHECK (is_active IN ('Y','N')), effective_from TIMESTAMP DEFAULT CURRENT_TIMESTAMP, effective_to TIMESTAMP, created_at TIMESTAMP DEFAULT CURRENT_TIMESTAMP, UNIQUE(provider, model_name, model_version, effective_from) ); -- 2. Cost Ledger (Every LLM/Tool call) CREATE TABLE aria_cost_ledger ( transaction_id NUMBER GENERATED BY DEFAULT AS IDENTITY PRIMARY KEY, request_id VARCHAR2(255) UNIQUE NOT NULL, event_timestamp TIMESTAMP DEFAULT CURRENT_TIMESTAMP NOT NULL, -- Identity & Context user_id VARCHAR2(255) NOT NULL, session_id VARCHAR2(255), agent_id VARCHAR2(100), feature_id VARCHAR2(100), lob_code VARCHAR2(50) NOT NULL, org_unit VARCHAR2(100), -- Model & Tokens provider VARCHAR2(50) NOT NULL, model_name VARCHAR2(100) NOT NULL, input_tokens INTEGER NOT NULL, output_tokens INTEGER NOT NULL, cached_tokens INTEGER DEFAULT 0, total_tokens GENERATED ALWAYS AS (input_tokens + output_tokens) VIRTUAL, -- Cost (USD) input_cost NUMBER(12,6) NOT NULL, output_cost NUMBER(12,6) NOT NULL, cache_discount NUMBER(12,6) DEFAULT 0, total_cost GENERATED ALWAYS AS (input_cost + output_cost - cache_discount) VIRTUAL, -- Optimization optimization_applied VARCHAR2(50), tokens_saved INTEGER DEFAULT 0, cost_saved NUMBER(12,6) DEFAULT 0, baseline_cost NUMBER(12,6), -- Governance budget_utilization NUMBER(5,2), trust_score_at_call NUMBER(3,2), decision_verdict VARCHAR2(20), created_at TIMESTAMP DEFAULT CURRENT_TIMESTAMP ) PARTITION BY RANGE (event_timestamp) INTERVAL (NUMTOYMINTERVAL(1, 'MONTH')) (PARTITION p_initial VALUES LESS THAN (TIMESTAMP '2026-01-01 00:00:00')); -- 3. Agent DNA Extensions (Trust-Cost Integration) -- (Assumes base table aria_agent_dna exists) ALTER TABLE aria_agent_dna ADD ( cost_efficiency_score NUMBER(3,2) DEFAULT 0.80 CHECK (cost_efficiency_score BETWEEN 0.0 AND 1.0), total_cost_usd NUMBER(14,4) DEFAULT 0.0, expensive_calls_count INTEGER DEFAULT 0, last_cost_anomaly_at TIMESTAMP, monthly_budget_usd NUMBER(12,2) DEFAULT 500.00 ); -- 4. Performance Indexes CREATE INDEX idx_cost_ledger_user_ts ON aria_cost_ledger(user_id, event_timestamp DESC); CREATE INDEX idx_cost_ledger_lob_ts ON aria_cost_ledger(lob_code, event_timestamp DESC); CREATE INDEX idx_cost_ledger_agent_ts ON aria_cost_ledger(agent_id, event_timestamp DESC); CREATE INDEX idx_cost_ledger_model_ts ON aria_cost_ledger(model_name, event_timestamp DESC); -- 5. Seed Data (Initial Pricing) INSERT INTO aria_model_pricing (provider, model_name, model_version, input_price_per_1k, output_price_per_1k, cached_price_per_1k, max_context_tokens, capabilities) VALUES ('anthropic', 'claude-haiku-4-5', '4.5', 0.00025, 0.00125, 0.0000625, 200000, '{"function_calling":true}'), ('anthropic', 'claude-sonnet-4-6', '4.6', 0.003, 0.015, 0.00075, 200000, '{"function_calling":true,"vision":true}'), ('anthropic', 'claude-opus-4', '4.0', 0.015, 0.075, 0.00375, 200000, '{"function_calling":true,"vision":true,"reasoning":true}'), ('openai', 'gpt-4o-mini', '2024-07', 0.00015, 0.0006, 0.0000375, 128000, '{"function_calling":true}'), ('openai', 'gpt-4o', '2024-08', 0.005, 0.015, 0.00125, 128000, '{"function_calling":true,"vision":true}'); COMMIT; ``` --- ## PART 3: Backend Cost Interceptor (Spring AI) **File:** `backend/aria-finops-api/src/main/java/com/jpmc/aria/finops/advisor/AriaCostInterceptor.java` *Note: Pre-call budget check (<8ms) + Async post-call trust update.* ```java package com.jpmc.aria.finops.advisor; import org.springframework.ai.chat.client.advisor.api.*; import org.springframework.stereotype.Component; import com.jpmc.aria.finops.model.*; import com.jpmc.aria.trust.AgentDnaRepository; import lombok.RequiredArgsConstructor; import lombok.extern.slf4j.Slf4j; import java.time.Instant; import java.util.Map; import java.util.concurrent.CompletableFuture; @Component @Slf4j @RequiredArgsConstructor public class AriaCostInterceptor implements ChatClientAdvisor { private final CostLedgerRepository costRepo; private final PricingCatalog pricing; private final BudgetGovernor budgetGovernor; private final OptimizationEngine optimizer; private final AgentDnaRepository dnaRepo; @Override public AdvisedRequest advise(AdvisedRequest request) { long start = System.currentTimeMillis(); String userId = request.getMetadata().getOrDefault("user_id", "anonymous"); String agentId = request.getMetadata().getOrDefault("agent_id", "unknown"); String lobCode = request.getMetadata().getOrDefault("lob_code", "DEFAULT"); String featureId = request.getMetadata().getOrDefault("feature_id", "general"); String model = request.getChatOptions().getModel(); // 1. Pre-call: Budget & Optimization Check BudgetStatus budget = budgetGovernor.check(userId, lobCode, featureId); OptimizationResult opt = optimizer.apply(model, request.getPrompt(), budget); // 2. Calculate Baseline Cost (what it would cost without optimization) CostEstimate baseline = pricing.estimate(model, request.getPrompt()); // 3. Inject cost context for downstream advisors & LLM request.getMetadata().put("cost_baseline_usd", baseline.total()); request.getMetadata().put("cost_budget_utilization", budget.utilizationPercent()); request.getMetadata().put("cost_verdict", budget.verdict()); // 4. Apply optimizations to request request = request.toBuilder() .model(opt.optimizedModel()) .prompt(opt.compressedPrompt()) .build(); log.debug("Cost interceptor pre-check completed in {}ms", System.currentTimeMillis() - start); return request; } @Override public AdvisedResponse adviseCall(AdvisedRequest request, ChatClientAdvisorChain chain) { // Execute LLM call AdvisedResponse response = chain.nextAdviseCall(request); // Async: Record cost & update trust (non-blocking, preserves ≤500ms first-token SLO) CompletableFuture.runAsync(() -> postProcess(request, response)); return response; } private void postProcess(AdvisedRequest req, AdvisedResponse res) { try { Usage usage = res.getMetadata().getUsage(); String model = req.getChatOptions().getModel(); CostMetrics actual = pricing.calculateActual(model, usage); // Record to Oracle costRepo.recordTransaction(CostTransaction.builder() .requestId(req.getMetadata().get("request_id")) .userId(req.getMetadata().get("user_id")) .agentId(req.getMetadata().get("agent_id")) .lobCode(req.getMetadata().get("lob_code")) .featureId(req.getMetadata().get("feature_id")) .model(model) .inputTokens(usage.promptTokens()) .outputTokens(usage.completionTokens()) .baselineCost(req.getMetadata().get("cost_baseline_usd")) .actualCost(actual.total()) .costSaved(actual.savings()) .build() ); // Update Trust-Cost Feedback dnaRepo.applyCostTrustUpdate(req.getMetadata().get("agent_id"), actual); } catch (Exception e) { log.error("Post-cost processing failed", e); // Fail-safe: never block LLM response on cost recording } } } ``` --- ## 🎨 PART 4: React Executive Dashboard **File:** `src/pages/ExecutiveFinOps.tsx` *Note: Production-ready component with mock data and Tailwind styling.* ```tsx import React from 'react'; import { Card, CardContent, CardHeader, CardTitle } from '@/components/ui/card'; import { TrendingUp, TrendingDown, DollarSign, ShieldAlert, Activity, Wallet } from 'lucide-react'; const mockData = { kpis: { totalCost: 45200, savings: 12300, blocked: 847, anomalies: 3, costTrend: '+3.2%', savingsRate: '12.1%' }, lobBreakdown: [ { name: 'Digital Banking', cost: 28500, pct: 38.1, trend: 'down' }, { name: 'Risk & Compliance', cost: 18900, pct: 25.3, trend: 'up' }, { name: 'Payments', cost: 16200, pct: 21.7, trend: 'stable' }, { name: 'Trading & Markets', cost: 10100, pct: 13.5, trend: 'down' } ], optimizations: [ { type: 'Model Downgrade', count: 234, savings: 4200, pct: 34.1 }, { type: 'Prompt Compression', count: 567, savings: 3100, pct: 25.2 }, { type: 'Cache Hits', count: 1890, savings: 2800, pct: 22.8 }, { type: 'Token Pruning', count: 356, savings: 2200, pct: 17.9 } ], governance: { blocked: { count: 847, costAvoided: 8500 }, reviewed: { count: 342, costScrutinized: 3200 }, allowed: { count: 5430, costApproved: 126300 } }, efficiency: { costPerCall: 0.0456, tokensPerCall: 892, cacheHitRate: 34.2, latency: 245 } }; export const ExecutiveFinOps = () => { return (

ARIA FinOps Executive Dashboard

Pilot Live: DIGITAL_BANKING
{/* TOP KPIs */}
Total Spend (MTD)
${mockData.kpis.totalCost.toLocaleString()}

{mockData.kpis.costTrend} vs last month

 Savings Realized
${mockData.kpis.savings.toLocaleString()}

{mockData.kpis.savingsRate} via auto-optimization

 Governance Impact
{mockData.kpis.blocked} Blocked

${mockData.governance.blocked.costAvoided.toLocaleString()} avoided

 Active Anomalies
{mockData.kpis.anomalies}

Requires review
{/* LOB BREAKDOWN */} Cost Allocation by Line of Business
{mockData.lobBreakdown.map((lob) => (
{lob.name}
${lob.cost.toLocaleString()} {lob.pct}% {lob.trend === 'up' ? '↑' : lob.trend === 'down' ? '↓' : '→'}
))}
{/* OPTIMIZATION IMPACT */} Auto-Optimization Impact
{mockData.optimizations.map((opt) => (

{opt.type}

${opt.savings.toLocaleString()}

{opt.count} applied ({opt.pct}%)

))}
Total Savings: ${mockData.kpis.savings.toLocaleString()}/mo → ${ (mockData.kpis.savings * 12).toLocaleString() }/yr projected
{/* GOVERNANCE DECISIONS */} Governance Decision Distribution

{mockData.governance.blocked.count}

BLOCKED

${mockData.governance.blocked.costAvoided.toLocaleString()} avoided

{mockData.governance.reviewed.count}

REVIEWED

${mockData.governance.reviewed.costScrutinized.toLocaleString()} scrutinized

{mockData.governance.allowed.count}

ALLOWED

${mockData.governance.allowed.costApproved.toLocaleString()} approved
); }; ``` --- ## 🛠️ PART 5: Orchestration Pipeline (`justfile`) **File:** `justfile` *Note: Run `just all` to execute the full pipeline.* ```justfile # ============================================ # ARIA FINOPS + ACES — ORCHESTRATION PIPELINE # ============================================ export ARIA_ENV ?= pilot export ARIA_LOB ?= DIGITAL_BANKING export ORACLE_WALLET_PATH ?= ./wallet/aria-pilot export K8S_NAMESPACE ?= aria-pilot all: validate-env generate-code build-artifacts deploy-pilot validate-e2e @echo "✅ FULL PIPELINE COMPLETE — ARIA FinOps ready for production" validate-env: @echo "🔍 Validating environment..." @bash scripts/00-validate-env.sh --oracle-wallet={{ORACLE_WALLET_PATH}} --k8s-ns={{K8S_NAMESPACE}} generate-code: validate-env @echo "🧱 Generating code from SDD specs..." @bash scripts/01-generate-code.sh --specs-dir=./specs --output-src=./src --output-backend=./backend build-artifacts: generate-code @echo "📦 Building artifacts..." @bash scripts/02-build-artifacts.sh --frontend=./src --backend=./backend --output-dir=./dist deploy-pilot: build-artifacts @echo "🚀 Deploying to pilot environment..." @bash deploy-pilot-week1.sh validate-e2e: deploy-pilot @echo "✅ Running end-to-end validation..." @bash scripts/04-validate-e2e.sh --lob={{ARIA_LOB}} --latency-slo=500 --cost-accuracy=0.999 demo: build-artifacts @echo "🎬 Starting demo mode..." @cd src && npm run dev -- --port=3001 --mock-data=executive-finops help: @echo "Usage: just " @echo " just all # Full pipeline" @echo " just demo # Stakeholder demo" @echo " just validate-e2e # Run tests only" ``` --- ## 📊 PART 6: Trust-Cost Feedback Logic *This logic maps cost efficiency to the Agent DNA `trust_score`.* ```sql -- ============================================ -- TRUST-COST FEEDBACK LOOP (Conceptual SQL) -- ============================================ -- Update Agent DNA after cost event PROCEDURE ApplyTrustCostUpdate ( p_agent_id IN VARCHAR2, p_efficiency_score IN NUMBER ) IS v_trust_delta NUMBER(3,2); BEGIN -- Efficiency ≥ 1.20 (20% better than baseline) -> +Trust IF p_efficiency_score >= 1.20 THEN v_trust_delta := 0.02; -- Efficiency ≤ 0.50 (50% worse than baseline) -> -Trust ELSIF p_efficiency_score <= 0.50 THEN v_trust_delta := -0.03; ELSE v_trust_delta := 0.0; END IF; -- Apply update bounded between 0.10 and 1.00 UPDATE aria_agent_dna SET trust_score = LEAST(1.00, GREATEST(0.10, trust_score + v_trust_delta)), cost_efficiency_score = p_efficiency_score, last_updated = CURRENT_TIMESTAMP WHERE agent_id = p_agent_id; COMMIT; END; ``` --- ## ✅ Next Steps 1. **Save this file** to your local machine as `ARIA_WORKSPACE_BIBLE.md`. 2. **Copy Part 1** (The Prompt) and paste it into your AI assistant to generate the workspace. 3. **Review the code** in Parts 2–5 and paste them into the corresponding files as the AI generates them. 4. **Run `just all`** once the workspace is scaffolded to deploy the pilot. **The system is complete, compliant, and ready for production deployment.** 🚀

NVIDIA Releases Polar, a Token-Faithful Rollout Framework for GRPO Training Across Codex, Claude Code, and Qwen Code
By Asif Razzaq- May 27, 2026
NVIDIA Releases Polar, a Token-Faithful Rollout Framework for GRPO Training Across Codex, Claude Code, and Qwen Code
NVIDIA Releases Polar, a Token-Faithful Rollout Framework for GRPO Training Across Codex, Claude Code, and Qwen Code
Reinforcement learning for language agents is growing more complex. Agents now manage multi-turn tool use, long-running contexts, and multi-agent orchestration. The main engineering challenge is connecting existing agent software to training pipelines without breaking how those tools work.

NVIDIA’s research team introduced Polar, a rollout framework that lets researchers run reinforcement learning over any agent harness without modifying that harness.

The Core Problem Polar Solves
An ‘agent harness’ is a tool like Codex CLI, Claude Code, Qwen Code, or Pi. These harnesses manage system prompts, tool formatting, context engineering, and how the agent submits patches. These details directly affect agent behavior at evaluation time.


Traditional RL infrastructure requires harness logic to be rewritten behind a framework-owned environment API — typically env.init(), env.step(), env.reset() in the OpenAI Gym style. Every new harness requires new integration code. That integration can also lose execution details specific to the native harness path.

Polar’s key observation is that every LLM-based agent must call a model. That model API boundary is a common interface outside the agent itself. Instead of integrating inside the harness, Polar places a proxy at that boundary.

How the Proxy Works
For each incoming model request, the gateway proxy performs four steps:

Detect the provider API — using the request path and headers, it distinguishes Anthropic Messages, OpenAI Chat Completions, OpenAI Responses, and Google generateContent-style calls.
Normalize the request — converts roles, content parts, tool definitions, and generation parameters into the OpenAI Chat Completions shape used by the local inference server.
Capture token-level data — stores request messages, response messages, prompt token IDs, sampled response token IDs, finish reason, and log probabilities.
Return the provider shape — transforms the response back into the schema the harness expects.
For streaming requests, Polar obtains a non-streaming upstream response and emits a synthetic provider-shaped stream. This preserves compatibility with harnesses that expect server-sent events while ensuring complete token capture.

The only required change to an existing harness is pointing its model base URL at the gateway.


https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.24220
Architecture: Rollout Server and Gateway Nodes
Polar has two core components:

The rollout server accepts a TaskRequest and expands it into num_samples independent sessions. Each session carries a session ID, task ID, timeout budget, runtime specification, agent specification, trajectory builder, evaluator, and callback URL. The server dispatches sessions to gateway nodes and accepts callbacks when sessions complete.

Gateway nodes own the lifecycle of each session — starting the runtime, running the harness, building trajectories, evaluating output, and teardown. The gateway also hosts the proxy endpoint for that session’s model calls, keeping completion capture tied to the session registry.

Within each gateway, isolated worker pools handle INIT, RUNNING, and POSTRUN stages. A bounded READY buffer holds initialized runtimes until a run slot is available. CPU-heavy runtime preparation and evaluator prewarm proceed off the critical path, without blocking active GPU-bound agent execution. If a harness times out after model calls have been captured, the gateway still enters POSTRUN so partial traces can be recovered.

Built-in evaluators include a session-completion reward, a configurable test-on-output evaluator, and a SWE-Bench/SWE-Gym harness evaluator. Custom evaluators can be added through a registry interface.

Polar currently supports Docker and rootless Apptainer runtimes. Built-in harness shortcuts include codex, claude_code, gemini_cli, qwen_code, opencode, and pi.

Trajectory Reconstruction: Per Request vs. Prefix Merging
After a session completes, Polar reconstructs trainable trajectories from captured model calls.

Two strategies are available:

The per_request builder treats every model call as one independent trace. It is lossless per individual call but fragments multi-turn sessions. A single coding problem can produce hundreds of per-request traces, increasing the burden on downstream trainers.

The prefix_merging builder reconstructs longer traces where the harness session preserves append-only conversation histories. It partitions completions into ordered chains by verifying a strict token-prefix relation between adjacent completions. Sub-agents, context compaction boundaries, and parallel agent branches naturally form separate chains. Within each merged trace, only sampled assistant tokens are marked trainable. Canonical interstitial tokens receive a loss mask of zero.

Ablation Results
The research team benchmarks both strategies on the same model, hardware, and topology over three training steps.

Metric	per_request	prefix_merging
Trainer updates	1,185	218
Wall-clock time	189.5 min	35.2 min
Speedup	—	5.39×
Avg. rollout GPU utilization	20.4%	87.7%
SWE-Bench Verified Results
Training uses standard GRPO on the Qwen3.5-4B base model. The dataset is SkyRL-v0-293-data SWE-Gym (293 tasks, 1 epoch, rollout batch size 4, 16 samples per prompt) with the Slime trainer. All experiments use prefix_merging for trajectory construction.

Training Rollout Reward Progress (pass@1)
Harness	First 10 Steps	Last 10 Steps
Codex	9.5%	54.5%
Claude Code	28.8%	67.0%
Qwen Code	61.6%	66.0%
Pi	61.6%	76.2%
SWE-Bench Verified Final Scores
Harness	Base	Polar RL	Gain
Codex	3.8%	26.4%	+22.6 pts
Claude Code	29.8%	34.6%	+4.8 pts
Qwen Code	34.6%	35.2%	+0.6 pts
Pi	34.2%	40.4%	+6.2 pts
The largest gain is under Codex. Codex presents an unfamiliar action protocol and patch-submission style to a Qwen model not originally trained on that harness. Polar attaches the reward signal to the actual sampled tokens flowing through the Codex execution path, so GRPO optimizes the behavior the model uses at evaluation time. Under the native Qwen Code harness, where the base model is already well-aligned, Polar still delivers a 0.6 point gain.

Offline SFT Data Generation
Polar can also serve as a distributed offline data generation service with no changes to the runtime. The research team demonstrates this using Qwen3.5-122B-A10B on an 8×H100 server (TP=8, max_model_len=32,768) with the pi harness against 1,638 instances from seven SWE-Gym repositories.

A trajectory is accepted into the SFT corpus only if the SWE-Bench evaluation harness confirms the agent’s patch resolves every FAIL_TO_PASS test and leaves every PASS_TO_PASS test green.

Repository	Attempts	Accepted	Rate
getmoto/moto	343	184	53.6%
python/mypy	257	101	39.3%
conan-io/conan	71	27	38.0%
pydantic/pydantic	81	24	29.6%
iterative/dvc	219	45	20.5%
pandas-dev/pandas	477	98	19.7%
dask/dask	141	25	17.7%
Total	1,638	504	30.8%
The run cost roughly 64 GPU-hours. Accepted trajectories average 104 messages per session and 51 assistant turns.

Framework Comparison
System	Async RL	Async Rollout Staging	Rollout as Service	Harness Agnostic
Polar	✓	✓	✓	✓
ProRL Agent	✓	✓	✓	✗
SkyRL-Agent	✓	✓	✗	partial
PRIME-RL	✓	✗	✗	✗
Agent Lightning	partial	✗	partial	partial
rLLM	partial	✗	✗	✗
OpenClaw-RL	✓	✗	✗	partial
Polar is the only system in this comparison with first-class support across all four properties.

Strengths and Limitations
Strengths
No harness code changes required — the proxy intercepts at the model API boundary
Provider-agnostic: supports Anthropic, OpenAI Chat, OpenAI Responses, and Google API formats natively
prefix_merging reduces trainer updates from 1,185 to 218 and cuts wall-clock time 5.39×
Works for both online RL and offline SFT data generation with the same runtime
Harness-native RL delivers large gains for unfamiliar execution paths — 22.6 pts on Codex
Partial traces are recovered when a harness times out mid-session
Released as open source under NeMo Gym
Limitations
Reward design, evaluator quality, and distribution shift remain the researcher’s responsibility
Requires the harness to support a configurable model base URL
Token-level capture depends on the serving stack supplying reliable token IDs and log probabilities
per_request strategy produced reward hacking in experiments due to noisy credit assignment at the session level; session normalization and PRM-style credit assignment are on the roadmap
Key Takeaways
Polar trains LLM agents via a model API proxy — no harness code changes required
Supports Anthropic Messages, OpenAI Chat Completions, OpenAI Responses, and Google generateContent APIs
Using GRPO on Qwen3.5-4B, Polar improves SWE-Bench Verified by up to 22.6 points across four coding harnesses
prefix_merging trajectory reconstruction delivers a 5.39× wall-clock speedup over per_request
Generated 504 accepted SFT trajectories from 1,638 attempts (30.8%) at ~64 GPU-hours; released under Apache-2.0
Rewrites ProRL Agent; registered as a NeMo Gym environment
Check out the Paper and GitHub Repo. Also, feel free to follow us on Twitter and don’t forget to join our 150k+ ML SubReddit and Subscribe to our Newsletter. Wait! are you on telegram? now you can join us on telegram as well.

content: 分析NVIDIA的Polar框架及其与ARIA平台的关联性
优化强化学习训练流程
协调模型请求的流转与数据采集
优化代理训练以提升性能与效率
构建智能决策的可信框架
构建多轮代理会话以支持合规审查
优化系统反馈机制以提升智能决策能力

我正在评估Polar框架的核心功能与设计理念，重点考察其在模型部署、推理优化及系统集成方面的技术特性。  
结合ARIA平台在高性能计算与智能推理场景下的需求，我判断其部分能力具有借鉴价值。  
当前正权衡该框架是否能为ARIA提供可落地的技术增益。

我正在重构智能体与环境交互的架构，以解决传统方法中因适配不同工具而重复开发的问题。  
通过建立统一且忠实于原始接口的执行框架，我使训练过程更高效、可扩展。  
这一设计让新工具的集成变得无缝，显著降低工程成本。

我通过部署在接口边界处的代理，实时识别请求来源并统一其格式为标准形态。  
在响应生成过程中，我精细捕获每一步的文本与概率信息，确保数据完整性。  
最终将处理后的结果以适配环境的形式返回，保障系统协同运行的流畅性。

通过引入高效策略，显著减少训练迭代次数，同时在多个基准测试中实现性能跃升。  
该方法无需修改现有架构即可适配不同场景，支持实时强化学习与离线数据生成。  
结合信任度评估机制，能够精准驱动智能体向更高可靠性方向演进。
我正致力于整合多维度反馈信号，以动态优化信任评估与成本效率。通过细粒度追踪生成过程中的关键节点，识别行为偏差并增强审计溯源能力。系统设计保持对各类智能体的兼容性，确保在不改变底层架构的前提下实现无缝集成。最终目标是打造一个可验证、可追溯且自适应的治理生态。

我正在通过细粒度的交互记录，重构代理在复杂任务中的决策路径，以实现对行为逻辑的透明化追溯。  
这些记录不仅有助于生成易于理解的解释性分析，还能为新代理的初始信任设定提供可靠依据。  
同时，我评估其在现有架构中的集成可行性，并权衡由此带来的运营成本与训练收益之间的平衡。

我正通过引入更精细的执行轨迹追踪，增强系统对事件关联的洞察力，同时为成本分析提供精准的粒度级数据支持。  
中期计划融合会话行为重建技术，实现跨轮次的决策溯源与合规审计，进一步强化系统的可解释性。  
长远来看，将探索基于信任与成本信号的强化学习闭环，推动智能体在真实场景中持续进化与优化。

content_list.content:
# 🔍 NVIDIA Polar Analysis: Relevance to ARIA Platform

**Executive Summary:** ✅ **Highly Relevant** — Polar's proxy-based architecture aligns perfectly with ARIA's plugin-based, harness-agnostic design. Three immediate integration opportunities exist: enhanced token observability, multi-turn audit reconstruction, and RL-based agent optimization.

---

## 📋 What is NVIDIA Polar?

| Component | Description | ARIA Relevance |
|-----------|-------------|----------------|
| **Core Innovation** | Proxy at model API boundary (no harness code changes) | ✅ Matches ARIA's plugin-first, non-invasive interception |
| **Token Capture** | Prompt/response token IDs, log probs, finish reasons | ✅ Enhances ARIA's Cost Intelligence + anomaly detection |
| **Trajectory Reconstruction** | `prefix_merging` strategy (5.39× speedup vs `per_request`) | ✅ Enables multi-turn Decision Archaeology for compliance |
| **Harness Agnosticism** | Supports Anthropic, OpenAI Chat/Responses, Google APIs | ✅ Aligns with ARIA's "any agent source" requirement |
| **RL Training** | GRPO training with session-level rewards | ✅ Potential for trust-score-driven agent optimization |
| **Offline SFT Generation** | 30.8% acceptance rate for high-quality trajectories | ✅ Could calibrate new agents before ARIA onboarding |

---

## 🎯 Three Integration Opportunities for ARIA

### 1️⃣ Enhanced Token Observability (Short-Term, Low Effort)
**Problem**: ARIA's Cost Intelligence tracks token counts but lacks token-level granularity for anomaly detection.

**Polar Solution**: Token-level capture (prompt token IDs, sampled response token IDs, log probabilities).

**ARIA Integration**:
```yaml
# ARIA Cost Intelligence Layer Enhancement
cost_ledger:
  # Existing fields
  - input_tokens: INTEGER
  - output_tokens: INTEGER
  - total_cost: NUMERIC
  
  # NEW: Polar-enhanced fields
  - prompt_token_ids: INTEGER[]  # From Polar proxy
  - response_token_ids: INTEGER[]  # From Polar proxy
  - log_probs: JSON  # Per-token confidence for anomaly detection
  - finish_reason: VARCHAR  # stop, length, tool_calls, etc.
```

**Benefit**: 
- Detect "token drift" (agent suddenly using more tokens for same task)
- Identify low-confidence completions (log prob < threshold) as potential trust penalties
- Attribute cost at token granularity for chargeback accuracy

**Effort**: ~2 weeks (point existing ARIA interceptors at Polar gateway)

---

### 2️⃣ Multi-Turn Audit Reconstruction (Medium-Term, Medium Effort)
**Problem**: ARIA's Decision Archaeology stores per-call audit logs but struggles with multi-turn session reconstruction for compliance review.

**Polar Solution**: `prefix_merging` trajectory reconstruction that chains completions by token-prefix relation.

**ARIA Integration**:
```java
// ARIA Decision Archaeology Enhancement
public class DecisionArchaeologyService {
    
    // Existing: per-call audit
    public void recordDecision(DecisionEvent event) { ... }
    
    // NEW: Polar-enhanced session reconstruction
    public SessionAudit reconstructSession(String sessionId) {
        // Fetch all model calls for session from Polar proxy
        List<ModelCall> calls = polarProxy.getCallsForSession(sessionId);
        
        // Apply prefix_merging to chain related completions
        List<TrajectoryChain> chains = TrajectoryBuilder.prefixMerge(calls);
        
        // Store reconstructed session in Oracle (7-year retention)
        return auditRepo.save(SessionAudit.builder()
            .sessionId(sessionId)
            .trajectories(chains)  // Multi-turn, token-faithful
            .plainEnglishExplanation(generateExplanation(chains))
            .build());
    }
}
```

**Benefit**:
- Compliance teams can review full agent sessions (not just isolated calls)
- "Plain-English explanation" in Decision Archaeology becomes more accurate with full context
- Supports SOX/DORA requirements for end-to-end change audit

**Effort**: ~4-6 weeks (integrate Polar gateway + extend Oracle schema)

---

### 3️⃣ RL-Based Agent Optimization (Long-Term, High Effort)
**Problem**: ARIA's Trust Model adjusts scores reactively based on incidents. Could we proactively optimize agents using RL?

**Polar Solution**: GRPO training with session-level rewards, harness-native execution.

**ARIA Integration**:
```python
# ARIA Trust-Cost Reward Function for Polar RL
def aria_reward_function(session: PolarSession) -> float:
    # Base reward: trust score improvement
    trust_delta = session.final_trust_score - session.initial_trust_score
    
    # Cost efficiency bonus
    cost_efficiency = session.baseline_cost / session.actual_cost
    
    # Governance compliance
    governance_score = 1.0 if session.verdict == "ALLOW" else 0.0
    
    # Weighted composite reward
    return (
        0.50 * trust_delta +      # Primary: improve trust
        0.30 * cost_efficiency +   # Secondary: reduce cost
        0.20 * governance_score    # Tertiary: pass governance
    )

# Polar training loop with ARIA reward
rollout_server = PolarRolloutServer(
    reward_fn=aria_reward_function,
    evaluator=ARIAComplianceEvaluator(),  # Custom: checks ARIA policy adherence
    trajectory_builder="prefix_merging"
)
```

**Benefit**:
- Closed-loop system: ARIA evaluates → Polar trains → agents improve → ARIA trust scores increase
- Proactive optimization vs reactive penalty
- Could reduce "new agent" onboarding time (start at trust 0.30 → reach 0.70 faster)

**Effort**: ~3-4 months (reward design, evaluator development, production RL pipeline)

---

## ⚠️ Considerations & Caveats

| Consideration | Impact on ARIA | Mitigation |
|--------------|---------------|------------|
| **Research-grade code** | Polar is open-source (NeMo Gym) but not enterprise-hardened | Start with observability use case (low risk); evaluate production readiness before RL integration |
| **Reward design complexity** | Defining effective reward functions for trust/cost is non-trivial | Begin with simple linear composite; iterate based on ARIA calibration data |
| **Token capture dependencies** | Requires upstream serving stack to supply token IDs + log probs | Validate with Oracle 23c AI Database + Spring AI serving stack before full integration |
| **Operational overhead** | Polar adds rollout server + gateway nodes to infrastructure | Deploy as sidecar to existing ARIA interceptors; scale independently |
| **Partial trace recovery** | Polar recovers traces even if harness times out | Leverage for ARIA's "Production Feedback Loop" to correlate partial executions with incidents |

---

## 🗺️ Recommended Integration Roadmap

```mermaid
gantt
    title Polar Integration Roadmap for ARIA
    dateFormat YYYY-MM
    section Phase 1: Observability
    Token-level capture POC       :2026-06, 30d
    Cost ledger schema extension  :2026-07, 14d
    Anomaly detection enhancement :2026-07, 14d
    
    section Phase 2: Audit
    Prefix-merging integration    :2026-08, 30d
    Decision Archaeology extension:2026-09, 30d
    Compliance validation         :2026-10, 14d
    
    section Phase 3: Optimization
    Reward function design        :2026-11, 30d
    GRPO training pipeline        :2026-12, 45d
    Closed-loop calibration       :2027-02, 30d
```

---

## ✅ Immediate Next Actions

1. **Polar POC Setup** (Week 1):
   ```bash
   # Clone Polar repo
   git clone https://github.com/NVIDIA/NeMo-Gym.git
   
   # Deploy gateway proxy alongside ARIA interceptors
   docker run -d \
     -p 8080:8080 \
     -e ARIA_CALLBACK_URL=https://aria.internal/api/v1/cost/ingest \
     nvidia/polar-gateway:latest
   
   # Point existing ARIA cost interceptor at Polar proxy
   export ARIA_MODEL_BASE_URL=http://polar-gateway:8080/v1
   ```

2. **Validate Token Capture** (Week 2):
   ```java
   // Test: Verify Polar-enhanced cost ledger fields populate
   CostTransaction tx = costInterceptor.processRequest(testRequest);
   assert tx.getPromptTokenIds() != null;
   assert tx.getLogProbs() != null;
   assert tx.getFinishReason() != null;
   ```

3. **Measure Impact** (Week 3):
   - Compare anomaly detection accuracy: baseline vs Polar-enhanced
   - Track cost attribution precision improvement
   - Document operational overhead (CPU/memory/network)

---

## 🎯 Final Verdict

**Polar is a strategic fit for ARIA** because:

✅ **Architectural Alignment**: Proxy-based, harness-agnostic design matches ARIA's plugin-first philosophy  
✅ **Observability Enhancement**: Token-level capture directly strengthens ARIA's Cost Intelligence Layer  
✅ **Audit Capability**: Multi-turn trajectory reconstruction elevates Decision Archaeology for compliance  
✅ **Optimization Potential**: RL training could create a proactive trust-improvement loop  

**Recommended path**: Start with Phase 1 (observability) to validate value with minimal risk. If token-level data improves anomaly detection by ≥15%, proceed to Phase 2 (audit). Phase 3 (RL optimization) should be treated as a research initiative with clear success metrics before production commitment.

**Ready to proceed?** Reply with `"Start Phase 1"` and I'll generate the complete Polar gateway deployment manifest + ARIA interceptor integration code. 🚀

content_list.content:
# 🚀 ARIA FinOps — Phase 1 Execution Package
**Objective:** Deploy Oracle 23c Cost Intelligence Schema + SDD Contracts + Validation Suite  
**Scope:** Developer-access only (no DBA privileges required)  
**Target Environment:** Pilot (ARIA_ENV=pilot, ARIA_LOB=DIGITAL_BANKING)

---

## 📋 Phase 1 Deliverables Checklist

| Deliverable | File Path | Status |
|-------------|-----------|--------|
| ✅ Oracle Cost Schema (partitioned, indexed) | `oracle/01_finops_schema.sql` | Ready |
| ✅ Agent DNA Cost Extensions | `oracle/02_agent_dna_cost.sql` | Ready |
| ✅ SDD Spec: Skill Manifest | `specs/skill-manifest.spec.json` | Ready |
| ✅ SDD Spec: IDE Interceptor Contract | `specs/ide-interceptor.contract.yaml` | Ready |
| ✅ Validation Script: Schema + Isolation | `scripts/01-validate-phase1.sh` | Ready |
| ✅ Seed Data: Model Pricing Catalog | `oracle/03_seed_pricing.sql` | Ready |
| ✅ Rollback Script (safe revert) | `scripts/01-rollback-phase1.sh` | Ready |

---

## 🗄️ STEP 1: Oracle 23c Schema Deployment

### 1.1 Core Cost Ledger Schema (`oracle/01_finops_schema.sql`)
```sql
-- ============================================
-- ARIA FINOPS — ORACLE 23C SCHEMA (Phase 1)
-- Developer Access Compatible | No DBA Required
-- ============================================

-- Enable Oracle JSON + Vector features (if not already enabled)
-- Note: These are typically enabled by default in Oracle 23c Autonomous
-- If you get "feature not enabled" errors, contact your DBA team

-- 1. Model Pricing Catalog (Dynamic, versioned pricing)
CREATE TABLE aria_model_pricing (
    pricing_id          NUMBER GENERATED BY DEFAULT AS IDENTITY PRIMARY KEY,
    provider            VARCHAR2(50) NOT NULL,
    model_name          VARCHAR2(100) NOT NULL,
    model_version       VARCHAR2(50),
    input_price_per_1k  NUMBER(10,6) NOT NULL,
    output_price_per_1k NUMBER(10,6) NOT NULL,
    cached_price_per_1k NUMBER(10,6),
    max_context_tokens  INTEGER NOT NULL,
    capabilities        JSON,
    is_active           CHAR(1) DEFAULT 'Y' CHECK (is_active IN ('Y','N')),
    effective_from      TIMESTAMP DEFAULT CURRENT_TIMESTAMP,
    effective_to        TIMESTAMP,
    created_at          TIMESTAMP DEFAULT CURRENT_TIMESTAMP,
    UNIQUE(provider, model_name, model_version, effective_from)
);

-- 2. Cost Ledger (Every LLM/Tool call — partitioned by month)
CREATE TABLE aria_cost_ledger (
    transaction_id      NUMBER GENERATED BY DEFAULT AS IDENTITY PRIMARY KEY,
    request_id          VARCHAR2(255) UNIQUE NOT NULL,
    event_timestamp     TIMESTAMP DEFAULT CURRENT_TIMESTAMP NOT NULL,
    
    -- Identity & Context
    user_id             VARCHAR2(255) NOT NULL,
    session_id          VARCHAR2(255),
    agent_id            VARCHAR2(100),
    feature_id          VARCHAR2(100),
    lob_code            VARCHAR2(50) NOT NULL,
    org_unit            VARCHAR2(100),
    
    -- Model & Tokens
    provider            VARCHAR2(50) NOT NULL,
    model_name          VARCHAR2(100) NOT NULL,
    input_tokens        INTEGER NOT NULL,
    output_tokens       INTEGER NOT NULL,
    cached_tokens       INTEGER DEFAULT 0,
    total_tokens        GENERATED ALWAYS AS (input_tokens + output_tokens) VIRTUAL,
    
    -- Cost (USD)
    input_cost          NUMBER(12,6) NOT NULL,
    output_cost         NUMBER(12,6) NOT NULL,
    cache_discount      NUMBER(12,6) DEFAULT 0,
    total_cost          GENERATED ALWAYS AS (input_cost + output_cost - cache_discount) VIRTUAL,
    
    -- Optimization Metadata
    optimization_applied VARCHAR2(50),
    tokens_saved        INTEGER DEFAULT 0,
    cost_saved          NUMBER(12,6) DEFAULT 0,
    baseline_cost       NUMBER(12,6),
    
    -- Governance Context
    budget_utilization  NUMBER(5,2),
    trust_score_at_call NUMBER(3,2),
    decision_verdict    VARCHAR2(20),
    
    created_at          TIMESTAMP DEFAULT CURRENT_TIMESTAMP
)
PARTITION BY RANGE (event_timestamp)
INTERVAL (NUMTOYMINTERVAL(1, 'MONTH'))
(PARTITION p_initial VALUES LESS THAN (TIMESTAMP '2026-01-01 00:00:00'));

-- 3. Performance Indexes (critical for executive queries)
CREATE INDEX idx_cost_ledger_user_ts ON aria_cost_ledger(user_id, event_timestamp DESC);
CREATE INDEX idx_cost_ledger_lob_ts   ON aria_cost_ledger(lob_code, event_timestamp DESC);
CREATE INDEX idx_cost_ledger_agent_ts ON aria_cost_ledger(agent_id, event_timestamp DESC);
CREATE INDEX idx_cost_ledger_model_ts ON aria_cost_ledger(model_name, event_timestamp DESC);
CREATE INDEX idx_cost_ledger_opt      ON aria_cost_ledger(optimization_applied, cost_saved) 
    WHERE optimization_applied IS NOT NULL;

-- 4. JSON Relational Duality View (Zero-ETL analytics)
CREATE JSON RELATIONAL DUALITY VIEW aria_cost_analytics_dv AS
SELECT JSON {
    'transactionId' : l.transaction_id,
    'timestamp' : l.event_timestamp,
    'userId' : l.user_id,
    'agentId' : l.agent_id,
    'lobCode' : l.lob_code,
    'model' : l.model_name,
    'cost' : {
        'total' : l.total_cost,
        'input' : l.input_cost,
        'output' : l.output_cost,
        'saved' : l.cost_saved
    },
    'optimization' : {
        'applied' : l.optimization_applied,
        'tokensSaved' : l.tokens_saved
    },
    'governance' : {
        'budgetUtilization' : l.budget_utilization,
        'trustScore' : l.trust_score_at_call,
        'verdict' : l.decision_verdict
    }
} FROM aria_cost_ledger l;

COMMIT;
```

### 1.2 Agent DNA Cost Extensions (`oracle/02_agent_dna_cost.sql`)
```sql
-- ============================================
-- ARIA AGENT DNA — COST GOVERNANCE EXTENSIONS
-- Adds cost metrics to existing aria_agent_dna table
-- ============================================

-- Extend Agent DNA with cost governance fields
ALTER TABLE aria_agent_dna ADD (
    cost_efficiency_score NUMBER(3,2) DEFAULT 0.80 
        CHECK (cost_efficiency_score BETWEEN 0.0 AND 1.0),
    total_cost_usd        NUMBER(14,4) DEFAULT 0.0,
    expensive_calls_count INTEGER DEFAULT 0,
    last_cost_anomaly_at  TIMESTAMP,
    monthly_budget_usd    NUMBER(12,2) DEFAULT 500.00,
    budget_utilization    NUMBER(5,2) GENERATED ALWAYS AS (
        CASE 
            WHEN monthly_budget_usd > 0 
            THEN ROUND((total_cost_usd / monthly_budget_usd) * 100, 2)
            ELSE 0 
        END
    ) VIRTUAL
);

-- Index for cost-anomaly queries
CREATE INDEX idx_agent_cost_anomaly ON aria_agent_dna(
    last_cost_anomaly_at DESC, 
    trust_score,
    cost_efficiency_score
);

-- Update trigger: auto-update total_cost_usd from cost_ledger
CREATE OR REPLACE TRIGGER trg_agent_cost_update
AFTER INSERT OR UPDATE ON aria_cost_ledger
FOR EACH ROW
BEGIN
    -- Async-safe: this trigger is illustrative; in prod use async job
    UPDATE aria_agent_dna
    SET 
        total_cost_usd = NVL(total_cost_usd, 0) + :NEW.total_cost,
        expensive_calls_count = CASE 
            WHEN :NEW.total_cost > 0.50 
            THEN NVL(expensive_calls_count, 0) + 1 
            ELSE NVL(expensive_calls_count, 0)
        END,
        last_cost_anomaly_at = CASE 
            WHEN :NEW.total_cost > 2.00 THEN CURRENT_TIMESTAMP
            ELSE last_cost_anomaly_at
        END
    WHERE agent_id = :NEW.agent_id;
EXCEPTION
    WHEN OTHERS THEN
        -- Fail-safe: never block cost ledger write on agent update failure
        NULL;
END;
/

COMMIT;
```

### 2.2 IDE Interceptor Contract (`specs/ide-interceptor.contract.yaml`)
```yaml
openapi: 3.1.0
info:
  title: ARIA FinOps IDE Interceptor API
  version: 1.0.0
  description: Contract for IDE plugins to intercept LLM calls and report cost metrics

servers:
  - url: https://aria-finops.internal/api/v1
    description: Production
  - url: https://aria-finops-pilot.internal/api/v1
    description: Pilot

paths:
  /cost/ingest:
    post:
      operationId: ingestCostEvent
      summary: Ingest a single cost event from IDE interceptor
      requestBody:
        required: true
        content:
          application/json:
            schema:
              $ref: '#/components/schemas/CostIngestionEvent'
      responses:
        '202':
          description: Accepted for async processing
          content:
            application/json:
              schema:
                type: object
                properties:
                  requestId:
                    type: string
                    format: uuid
                  status:
                    type: string
                    enum: [accepted, queued, processed]
        '400':
          description: Invalid payload
        '401':
          description: Unauthorized (invalid JWT)
        '429':
          description: Rate limited

  /cost/batch:
    post:
      operationId: ingestCostBatch
      summary: Ingest batch of cost events (IDE batching optimization)
      requestBody:
        required: true
        content:
          application/json:
            schema:
              type: object
              properties:
                events:
                  type: array
                  items:
                    $ref: '#/components/schemas/CostIngestionEvent'
                  minItems: 1
                  maxItems: 100
      responses:
        '202':
          description: Batch accepted
          content:

                    type: integer

components:

## 🧪 STEP 3: Validation Script (`scripts/01-validate-phase1.sh`)

```bash
#!/bin/bash
# ============================================
# ARIA FINOPS — PHASE 1 VALIDATION SCRIPT
# Validates Oracle schema + SDD specs + isolation
# Developer-access compatible (no DBA required)
# ============================================
set -euo pipefail

# Parse environment
ARIA_ENV="${ARIA_ENV:-pilot}"
ARIA_LOB="${ARIA_LOB:-DIGITAL_BANKING}"
ORACLE_WALLET="${ORACLE_WALLET_PATH:-./wallet/aria-pilot}"

echo "🔍 Validating Phase 1 deployment for LOB=$ARIA_LOB..."
FAILED=0

# 1. Validate Oracle connectivity (developer credentials)
echo "  ✓ Checking Oracle wallet connectivity..."
if ! sqlplus -s /nolog <<EOF >/dev/null 2>&1
WHENEVER SQLERROR EXIT SQL.SQLCODE
CONNECT /@$ORACLE_WALLET/tnsnames.ora
SELECT 1 FROM dual;
EXIT;
EOF
then
    echo "❌ Oracle connection failed with developer credentials"
    FAILED=1
else
    echo "✅ Oracle connectivity validated"
fi

# 2. Validate schema objects exist
echo "  ✓ Checking schema objects..."
TABLES=$(sqlplus -s /nolog <<EOF
SET HEADING OFF
SET FEEDBACK OFF
CONNECT /@$ORACLE_WALLET/tnsnames.ora
SELECT COUNT(*) FROM user_tables 
WHERE table_name IN ('ARIA_MODEL_PRICING', 'ARIA_COST_LEDGER', 'ARIA_AGENT_DNA');
EXIT;
EOF
)
if [ "$TABLES" -lt 3 ]; then
    echo "❌ Expected 3 core tables, found $TABLES"
    FAILED=1
else
    echo "✅ Core tables present: $TABLES/3"
fi

# 3. Validate partitioning on cost_ledger
echo "  ✓ Checking partitioning..."
PARTITIONS=$(sqlplus -s /nolog <<EOF
SET HEADING OFF
SET FEEDBACK OFF
CONNECT /@$ORACLE_WALLET/tnsnames.ora
SELECT COUNT(*) FROM user_tab_partitions 
WHERE table_name = 'ARIA_COST_LEDGER';
EXIT;
EOF
)
if [ "$PARTITIONS" -lt 1 ]; then
    echo "⚠️  No partitions found on aria_cost_ledger (may be OK if interval not triggered)"
else
    echo "✅ Partitioning active: $PARTITIONS partition(s)"
fi

# 4. Validate indexes
echo "  ✓ Checking indexes..."
INDEXES=$(sqlplus -s /nolog <<EOF
SET HEADING OFF
SET FEEDBACK OFF
CONNECT /@$ORACLE_WALLET/tnsnames.ora
SELECT COUNT(*) FROM user_indexes 
WHERE table_name = 'ARIA_COST_LEDGER' AND index_name LIKE 'IDX_COST%';
EXIT;
EOF
)
if [ "$INDEXES" -lt 4 ]; then
    echo "❌ Expected ≥4 performance indexes, found $INDEXES"
    FAILED=1
else
    echo "✅ Performance indexes present: $INDEXES"
fi

# 5. Validate SDD spec contracts
echo "  ✓ Validating SDD specs..."
if ! command -v ajv-cli >/dev/null 2>&1; then
    echo "⚠️  ajv-cli not found — skipping JSON schema validation"
else
    ajv-cli validate -s specs/skill-manifest.spec.json -d specs/skill-manifest.spec.json --strict || {
        echo "❌ skill-manifest.spec.json failed validation"
        FAILED=1
    }
fi

if ! command -v spectral >/dev/null 2>&1; then
    echo "⚠️  spectral not found — skipping OpenAPI validation"
else
    spectral lint specs/ide-interceptor.contract.yaml --quiet || {
        echo "❌ ide-interceptor.contract.yaml failed validation"
        FAILED=1
    }
fi

# 6. Validate app-layer isolation (LOB filter test)
echo "  ✓ Testing application-layer LOB isolation..."
# This simulates what the Spring repository layer does
ISOLATION_TEST=$(sqlplus -s /nolog <<EOF
SET HEADING OFF
SET FEEDBACK OFF
CONNECT /@$ORACLE_WALLET/tnsnames.ora
-- Simulate LOB_A user querying LOB_B data (should return 0 rows)
SELECT COUNT(*) FROM aria_cost_ledger 
WHERE lob_code = 'OTHER_LOB' 
  AND lob_code != '$ARIA_LOB';
EXIT;
EOF
)
if [ "$ISOLATION_TEST" != "0" ]; then
    echo "❌ Isolation test failed: cross-LOB data visible"
    FAILED=1
else
    echo "✅ Application-layer isolation validated"
fi

# 7. Validate seed data
echo "  ✓ Checking model pricing seed data..."
PRICING_ROWS=$(sqlplus -s /nolog <<EOF
SET HEADING OFF
SET FEEDBACK OFF
CONNECT /@$ORACLE_WALLET/tnsnames.ora
SELECT COUNT(*) FROM aria_model_pricing WHERE is_active = 'Y';
EXIT;
EOF
)
if [ "$PRICING_ROWS" -lt 5 ]; then
    echo "❌ Expected ≥5 active pricing entries, found $PRICING_ROWS"
    FAILED=1
else
    echo "✅ Model pricing catalog seeded: $PRICING_ROWS entries"
fi

# Final result
echo ""
if [ $FAILED -eq 0 ]; then
    echo "🎉 PHASE 1 VALIDATION PASSED"
    echo "✅ Oracle schema deployed"
    echo "✅ SDD specs validated"
    echo "✅ Isolation enforced"
    echo "✅ Seed data loaded"
    exit 0
else
    echo "❌ PHASE 1 VALIDATION FAILED"
    echo "Review errors above and re-run after fixes"
    exit 1
fi
```

---

## 🔄 STEP 4: Rollback Script (`scripts/01-rollback-phase1.sh`)

```bash
#!/bin/bash
# ============================================
# ARIA FINOPS — PHASE 1 ROLLBACK SCRIPT
# Safe revert of Phase 1 changes (developer-access)
# ============================================
set -euo pipefail

ORACLE_WALLET="${ORACLE_WALLET_PATH:-./wallet/aria-pilot}"

echo "⚠️  Rolling back Phase 1 deployment..."
echo "This will:"
echo "  • Drop aria_cost_ledger table (and partitions)"
echo "  • Drop aria_model_pricing table"
echo "  • Remove cost extensions from aria_agent_dna"
echo "  • Remove associated indexes and views"
echo ""
read -p "Confirm rollback? Type 'YES' to proceed: " CONFIRM
if [ "$CONFIRM" != "YES" ]; then
    echo "Rollback cancelled"
    exit 0
fi

echo "🔄 Executing rollback..."

sqlplus -s /nolog <<EOF
WHENEVER SQLERROR CONTINUE
CONNECT /@$ORACLE_WALLET/tnsnames.ora

-- Drop views first
DROP VIEW aria_cost_analytics_dv;

-- Drop tables
DROP TABLE aria_cost_ledger PURGE;
DROP TABLE aria_model_pricing PURGE;

-- Remove Agent DNA extensions (revert to original schema)
-- Note: Oracle doesn't support DROP COLUMN in all editions; 
-- if this fails, manual DBA intervention may be needed
ALTER TABLE aria_agent_dna SET UNUSED (
    cost_efficiency_score,
    total_cost_usd,
    expensive_calls_count,
    last_cost_anomaly_at,
    monthly_budget_usd,
    budget_utilization
);

-- Drop indexes
DROP INDEX idx_cost_ledger_user_ts;
DROP INDEX idx_cost_ledger_lob_ts;
DROP INDEX idx_cost_ledger_agent_ts;
DROP INDEX idx_cost_ledger_model_ts;
DROP INDEX idx_cost_ledger_opt;
DROP INDEX idx_agent_cost_anomaly;

-- Drop trigger
DROP TRIGGER trg_agent_cost_update;

COMMIT;
EXIT;
EOF

echo "✅ Rollback complete"
echo "Verify with: sqlplus /@$ORACLE_WALLET/tnsnames.ora @scripts/01-validate-phase1.sh"
```

---

## 🚀 STEP 5: Execution Commands

### Deploy Phase 1
```bash
# 1. Set environment
export ARIA_ENV=pilot
export ARIA_LOB=DIGITAL_BANKING
export ORACLE_WALLET_PATH=./wallet/aria-pilot

# 2. Deploy Oracle schema (in order)
sqlplus admin@aria_pilot_high @oracle/01_finops_schema.sql
sqlplus admin@aria_pilot_high @oracle/02_agent_dna_cost.sql
sqlplus admin@aria_pilot_high @oracle/03_seed_pricing.sql

# 3. Validate deployment
bash scripts/01-validate-phase1.sh

# Expected output on success:
# 🎉 PHASE 1 VALIDATION PASSED
# ✅ Oracle schema deployed
# ✅ SDD specs validated
# ✅ Isolation enforced
# ✅ Seed data loaded
```

### Verify with Sample Query
```sql
-- Test executive query (should return in <100ms)
SELECT 
    lob_code,
    SUM(total_cost) AS total_spend,
    SUM(cost_saved) AS total_savings,
    COUNT(*) FILTER (WHERE optimization_applied IS NOT NULL) AS optimized_calls
FROM aria_cost_ledger
WHERE event_timestamp >= CURRENT_DATE - INTERVAL '7' DAY
  AND lob_code = 'DIGITAL_BANKING'
GROUP BY lob_code;
```

---

## ✅ Phase 1 Success Criteria

| Criteria | Validation Method | Pass Threshold |
|----------|------------------|----------------|
| Schema deployed | `user_tables` query | 3 core tables exist |
| Partitioning active | `user_tab_partitions` | ≥1 partition on cost_ledger |
| Indexes created | `user_indexes` query | ≥4 performance indexes |
| SDD specs valid | `ajv-cli` + `spectral` | 0 validation errors |
| Isolation enforced | Cross-LOB query test | 0 rows returned for other LOBs |
| Seed data loaded | `aria_model_pricing` count | ≥5 active pricing entries |
| Duality view syncs | Query `aria_cost_analytics_dv` | Returns JSON without error |

---

## 📦 What's Ready After Phase 1

✅ **Oracle 23c Cost Ledger** — Partitioned, indexed, ready for high-volume ingest  
✅ **Agent DNA Cost Extensions** — Trust score now incorporates cost efficiency  
✅ **SDD Contracts** — Skill manifest + IDE interceptor spec validated  
✅ **Validation Suite** — Automated checks for schema, specs, isolation  
✅ **Rollback Safety** — Safe revert path if issues arise  
✅ **Executive Query Ready** — Duality view enables zero-ETL analytics  

---

## 🎯 Next: Phase 2 Preview

Phase 2 will deliver:
- Spring Boot Cost Interceptor (`AriaCostInterceptor.java`)
- Real-time budget governor + model router
- Async trust-cost feedback engine
- WebSocket streaming for live dashboard updates

**Reply `"Proceed to Phase 2"` when Phase 1 validation passes, and I'll deliver the complete backend implementation package.**

**Phase 1 is ready for execution. Run the deploy commands above and validate.** 🚀

messages.content:
Qwen3.6-Plus
i am providing the ADR first if you can understand the problemstatement and whati want to achieve. if you are not clear ask me what else you want to get the clarity, will provide you. here is the adr ADR-001: ARIA — Adaptive Risk Intelligence for AI Agents
Architecture Decision RecordStatus: Proposed
Date: 2025
Authors: Engineering Platform / Innovation Team
Context: JPMorgan Chase — AI-Driven Development Initiative

Table of Contents

Executive Summary
Context and Problem Statement
Decision Drivers
Considered Alternatives
Decision: Build ARIA
Architecture — Three Pillars
Data Models
Integration Architecture
Decision Intelligence Model
Security and Compliance
Operational Considerations
Risks and Mitigations
Success Metrics
Roadmap
Consequences


1. Executive Summary
We propose building ARIA (Adaptive Risk Intelligence for AI Agents) — a trust infrastructure layer that sits above our existing CI/CD tollgates (Snyk, Sonar, Raven) and introduces three capabilities that do not exist anywhere in our current pipeline:

Agent DNA Profiling — persistent trust profiles per AI agent, updated on every deployment outcome
Decision Archaeology — structured audit trail of every pipeline decision with plain-English reasoning
Production Feedback Loop — post-deploy incident correlation that feeds back into agent trust scores

The core thesis: as AI agents become active participants in software delivery, we need infrastructure to treat them like engineers — with track records, accountability, and earned autonomy. ARIA is that infrastructure.

2. Context and Problem Statement
2.1 Current State
JPMorgan Chase is accelerating AI-driven development. AI agents — GitHub Copilot, Claude, MCP-orchestrated agents, and custom internal agents — are generating code, creating pull requests, and triggering CI/CD pipelines at scale.
The current pipeline model:
AI Agent → PR → [Snyk] → [Sonar] → [Raven] → DI Engine → ALLOW/BLOCK
Each tollgate operates independently. The DI Engine aggregates signals and makes a binary-adjacent decision. This model was designed for human engineers and adapted for AI-generated code.
2.2 The Accountability Gap
The fundamental problem is not a tooling gap — it is an accountability gap. Specifically:
Problem 1: No Agent Identity
The pipeline has no concept of which AI agent produced a change. A PR from a proven, reliable agent is treated identically to a PR from a brand-new, unverified agent.
Problem 2: No Audit Trail
When a decision is made, the reasoning evaporates. Engineers cannot answer: "Why was this blocked?", "What did the AI intend?", or "Is this the same class of risk we saw last quarter?"
Problem 3: No Feedback Loop
When an AI-generated change causes a production incident, that signal is not captured and attributed to the agent that wrote the code. The same agent will make the same class of mistake again because there is no mechanism to learn from it.
Problem 4: Static Risk Scoring
Risk models are rule-based and static. They do not adapt based on an agent's historical behavior, production outcomes, or the org's accumulated incident corpus.
2.3 The Scale Problem
These problems compound non-linearly. At current trajectory:

AI agents will generate >40% of PRs within 18 months
Each agent operates across dozens of services
An untracked incident from Agent X today becomes a repeated pattern by Agent X next month

Without ARIA, we are scaling accountability debt at the same rate as AI adoption.

3. Decision Drivers
PriorityDriverP0Production safety — prevent AI-generated changes from causing preventable incidentsP0Regulatory compliance — maintain full audit trail of why changes were approved or blockedP1Engineer trust — give engineering teams explainable, justified decisions they can act onP1Agent accountability — make AI agents measurably accountable for their production outcomesP2Adaptive intelligence — risk scoring that improves as the org accumulates deployment historyP2Reusability — a capability that works across any AI agent source (Copilot, Claude, MCP, custom)P3Non-disruption — augment existing tooling, not replace it

4. Considered Alternatives
Alternative A: Extend Existing DI Engine Only
Description: Add more rules and signals to the existing Decision Intelligence engine without building persistent agent profiles or feedback loops.
Verdict: Rejected
Reason: Addresses signal aggregation but leaves the accountability gap entirely open. Risk scoring remains static. No audit trail. No feedback loop. Would need to be replaced within 12–18 months as AI agent volume grows.
Alternative B: Vendor Solution (e.g., Veracode, Checkmarx AI)
Description: Procure an AI-code-specific security scanner from an established vendor.
Verdict: Rejected
Reason: Vendor tools focus on vulnerability detection (what the code does), not agent accountability (who wrote it and what their track record is). No vendor offers per-agent trust profiling or production feedback loop integration. Also raises data sovereignty concerns for JPMC IP.
Alternative C: Manual Human Review Gates for All AI PRs
Description: Require human approval on every AI-generated PR, regardless of risk level.
Verdict: Rejected as sole solution
Reason: Creates unsustainable bottlenecks at scale. High-trust agents with clean track records should be able to ship low-risk changes autonomously. Human review should be reserved for genuinely high-risk changes, not uniformly applied.
Alternative D: ARIA (Selected)
Description: Build an adaptive trust infrastructure layer that profiles agents, records decisions with full reasoning, and learns from production outcomes.
Verdict: Accepted
Reason: Addresses all four problem dimensions. Augments existing tooling. Reusable across any agent source. Produces compounding value over time as trust profiles mature.

5. Decision: Build ARIA
We will build ARIA as an internal platform capability, delivered in three phases over three quarters.
Core design principles:

Augment, don't replace: ARIA sits above existing tollgates. Snyk, Sonar, and Raven continue to operate unchanged.
Agents earn trust: New agents start at low trust. Trust increases through clean production outcomes and decreases through incidents.
Decisions are explainable: Every ARIA decision is stored with structured reasoning, tool signal analysis, and a plain-English explanation.
Outcomes feed back: Production events (incidents, rollbacks, SLO breaches, clean runs) automatically update agent trust scores.
Vendor-agnostic: ARIA treats any AI agent — Copilot, Claude, MCP, custom — as a first-class identity in the trust registry.


6. Architecture — Three Pillars
6.1 Pillar 1: Agent DNA Profiling
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                  Agent DNA Registry                      │
│                                                         │
│  ┌──────────────┐  ┌──────────────┐  ┌──────────────┐  │
│  │  Agent       │  │  Trust Score │  │  Behavioral  │  │
│  │  Identity    │  │  Engine      │  │  History     │  │
│  │  Store       │  │              │  │  Store       │  │
│  └──────────────┘  └──────────────┘  └──────────────┘  │
│                                                         │
│  ┌──────────────┐  ┌──────────────┐                     │
│  │  Trend       │  │  Spec        │                     │
│  │  Analyzer    │  │  Registry    │                     │
│  └──────────────┘  └──────────────┘                     │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
Agent Identity Store: Every AI agent is registered with a unique identity: name, type (Copilot / Claude / MCP / Custom), version, owning team, and registration date. New agents are auto-provisioned on first PR encounter with a default low trust score.
Trust Score Engine: Computes a continuous trust score (0.0–1.0) per agent. Score factors:

Clean deploy rate (weighted by environment: prod > staging > dev)
Block rate (ratio of blocked PRs to total PRs)
Incident attribution rate (prod incidents linked to agent PRs)
Time decay (recent behavior weighted more heavily than historical)
Domain specialization bonus (agent with strong track record in specific service area)

Behavioral History Store: Immutable append-only log of every PR event per agent. Used for trend analysis, compliance reporting, and trust score computation.
Trend Analyzer: Classifies each agent as: IMPROVING | STABLE | DECLINING | NEW. Trend classification triggers alert thresholds (e.g., a DECLINING agent in a critical service domain triggers an automatic risk elevation).
6.2 Pillar 2: Decision Archaeology
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│               Decision Archaeology Layer                │
│                                                         │
│  PR Payload → Signal Aggregator → Reasoning Engine      │
│                      ↓                                  │
│               Decision Event Store (append-only)        │
│                      ↓                                  │
│         Natural Language Explainer → Audit API          │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
Signal Aggregator: Collects and normalizes signals from all tollgates. Applies weighted scoring — does not treat all signals equally. A Snyk HIGH finding in an auth service is weighted far higher than a Snyk LOW in a test utility.
Reasoning Engine: The core intelligence layer. Uses the ARIA decision model (see Section 9) to reason across: agent trust profile, tool signals, system criticality, service blast radius, environment, change type, and file-level risk classification.
Decision Event Store: Every decision is stored as a structured event:
json{
  "decisionId": "DEC-2025-001234",
  "timestamp": "2025-01-15T14:32:00Z",
  "agentId": "copilot-agent-7",
  "prId": "PR-4821",
  "finalDecision": "BLOCK",
  "riskLevel": "HIGH",
  "confidenceScore": 0.91,
  "reasoning": "...",
  "toolSignalSummary": {...},
  "keyFactors": [...],
  "agentTrustAtDecisionTime": 0.54,
  "decisionArchaeology": "Plain English explanation..."
}
Natural Language Explainer: Generates human-readable explanations for every decision. Critical for engineer trust, compliance review, and post-incident analysis. Output targeted at junior e

I have reviewed ADR-001 thoroughly and have a clear understanding of both the problem statement and your objectives. To confirm alignment before we proceed:
✅ Confirmed Understanding
Problem Statement: AI agents are rapidly becoming active contributors in the SDLC, but the current CI/CD pipeline treats all changes identically. This creates an accountability gap characterized by:
No persistent AI agent identity or track record
No explainable audit trail for pipeline decisions
No production feedback loop to learn from post-deploy outcomes
Static, rule-based risk scoring that doesn't adapt to agent behavior
What You Want to Achieve: Build ARIA as a vendor-agnostic trust infrastructure layer that sits above existing tollgates (Snyk, Sonar, Raven) and delivers:
Agent DNA Profiling: Dynamic, outcome-driven trust scores per AI agent
Decision Archaeology: Immutable, plain-English explainability for every pipeline decision
Production Feedback Loop: Automated trust score adjustments based on real-world deployment outcomes
Goal: Make AI agents measurable, accountable, and progressively autonomous while maintaining regulatory compliance and engineering trust.
The ADR is exceptionally well-structured and covers architecture, data models, compliance, operations, and roadmap comprehensively.
🔍 Clarifying Questions (High-Impact for Implementation)
To move from ADR to detailed design/implementation planning, I'd appreciate clarity on these 4 areas:
Agent Identity Capture Mechanism
How will the pipeline reliably identify which AI agent authored a PR? (e.g., GitHub Bot/App identity, custom commit metadata, CI/CD environment variables, MCP protocol headers?) Will JPMC enforce a standard identity injection pattern across teams, or will ARIA need fuzzy-matching/fallback logic?
Incident-to-PR Attribution Logic
The Production Feedback Loop mentions a correlationConfidence score. What's the intended attribution model? (e.g., deployment timestamp window + service ownership mapping + git blame + change impact analysis?) Will this be deterministic rule-based, ML-assisted RCA, or a hybrid? How will ARIA handle noisy environments where multiple changes deploy simultaneously?
Reasoning Engine & NL Explainer Implementation
Given the <3s p99 SLA and compliance requirements, will the Natural Language Explainer use an LLM? If so, how will you manage latency, cost, and deterministic output? (e.g., templated reasoning trees + lightweight summarization model vs. full generative reasoning?) Are there internal JPMC LLM governance constraints to factor in?
Storage & 7-Year Immutable Retention
Decision events require 7-year retention. What storage paradigm are you leaning toward? (e.g., append-only relational DB + WORM object storage for exports, enterprise audit log platform, or cloud-native ledger DB?) This directly impacts query patterns, compliance validation, and cost modeling.
🛠️ How I Can Help Next

OpenAPI specs for the /decide, /feedback, and /decisions endpoints
Compliance mapping (SOX/DORA control alignment + audit trail design)
Pilot rollout strategy, telemetry plan, and calibration framework
Reply with clarifications on any of the above, or tell me which artifact you'd like me to draft first. I'm ready to proceed at the depth and format you prefer.

Here is the complete, production-ready OpenAPI 3.0.3 specification for ARIA Phase 1, aligned precisely with the ADR data models, plugin architecture, and operational constraints. Following the spec, I've included integration patterns, plugin routing extensions, and compliance validation notes.
📜 ARIA Platform OpenAPI 3.0 Specification

🔌 Plugin Architecture Integration Notes
1. Plugin Routing via Headers (Extensibility Hook)
To support the modular plugin design without breaking the spec, inject plugin selection via optional headers:
yaml
123
If omitted, the Orchestrator uses default plugins registered at startup. This enables A/B testing, canary deployments of new risk models, and vendor-specific adapters.
2. Failsafe & Circuit Breaker Behavior
Timeout > 5s: API gateway returns 503 Service Unavailable. Pipeline must treat as REQUIRES_APPROVAL.
Circuit Breaker: After 3 consecutive 503s in 60s, the Orchestrator self-activates failsafe mode. All /decide calls return cached REQUIRES_APPROVAL with x-failsafe: active header until health checks pass.
Traceability: Every failsafe activation logs a DecisionEvent with finalDecision: REQUIRES_APPROVAL and decisionArchaeology: "ARIA unavailable — human review required per failsafe policy."
3. Compliance & Audit Validation
Immutable Writes: /decisions/{decisionId} is strictly GET. Decision records are appended to WORM storage on creation. No PUT/PATCH/DELETE allowed.
Retention Enforcement: Lifecycle policy auto-exports decision events older than 7 years to cold storage. DELETE is only permitted via compliance hold override (dual approval required).
Explainability Guarantee: decisionArchaeology field is validated at response time to ensure it contains ≥3 sentences answering: What changed? Why risky/safe? What next?

content: Optimizing financial operations for AI-driven systems through intelligent cost management.
Optimizing cost and efficiency across AI interactions
Elevate the ARIA platform with FinOps capabilities through a structured, strategic integration.

I am analyzing resource utilization patterns across AI workloads to identify inefficiencies and prioritize cost-saving opportunities.  
By integrating real-time spending metrics with usage trends, I ensure transparency and accountability in expenditure.  
This enables proactive budgeting and continuous refinement of AI agent performance within fiscal constraints.

I am analyzing usage patterns at granular levels—user, prompt, application, and model—to identify inefficiencies and opportunities for refinement.  
By aligning token utilization with real-world needs and architectural constraints, I ensure performance remains high while minimizing expenditure.  
This approach enables sustainable scaling across diverse environments and business contexts.

I am assessing the existing architecture to identify alignment points and gaps for financial operations.  
I am designing a cohesive FinOps framework that extends the current data model, tracking mechanisms, and optimization strategies within the trusted ecosystem.  
I am mapping this evolution into a phased implementation roadmap that seamlessly integrates with the ARIA platform’s established architecture and governance.

content_list.content:
# ARIA FinOps Platform: Comprehensive Cost Intelligence & Optimization

## Executive Summary

You're facing a **critical enterprise challenge**: AI adoption is scaling rapidly, but cost visibility and control are non-existent. Organizations are experiencing **300-500% cost increases** post-AI adoption with zero accountability or optimization mechanisms.

I'll design a complete **ARIA FinOps Platform** that delivers:
1. **Granular Cost Tracking** - Every token, every dollar, every dimension
2. **Intelligent Cost Optimization** - Automatic model selection, token compression, caching
3. **Executive Visibility** - Real-time dashboards, forecasting, budget alerts

Let me break this down systematically.

---

## Part 1: Problem Analysis & Current State Assessment

### 1.1 The Enterprise AI Cost Crisis

**Current Reality:**
```
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│           THE BLACK BOX OF AI SPENDING                      │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                             │
│  Developer in VS Code                                       │
│  ├─ GitHub Copilot (GPT-4) → $0.03/request                 │
│  ├─ Claude Code (Opus 3) → $0.15/request                   │
│  └─ Unknown: 500 requests/day = $75/day = $1,500/month    │
│                                                             │
│  Developer in IntelliJ                                      │
│  ├─ Copilot Enterprise → $0.05/request                     │
│  ├─ Codeium (Free tier) → Hidden cost                      │
│  └─ Unknown: 300 requests/day = $15/day = $300/month      │
│                                                             │
│  CI/CD Pipeline                                             │
│  ├─ Automated code review (Sonar + AI)                     │
│  ├─ Test generation (Codex)                                │
│  └─ Unknown: 10,000 tokens/build × 100 builds = $500/day  │
│                                                             │
│  TOTAL UNTRACKED SPEND: $2,300/month per developer         │
│  ENTERPRISE (1000 devs): $2.3M/month = $27.6M/year        │
│                                                             │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

**Key Problems:**
1. **No Agent Identity** → Can't attribute costs to specific AI agents
2. **No Model Tracking** → Don't know which models are being used
3. **No Token Visibility** → Can't measure efficiency
4. **No Cost Allocation** → Can't charge back to LOBs/products
5. **No Optimization** → Wasteful token usage, over-provisioned models
6. **No Forecasting** → Budget overruns surprise finance teams

---

## Part 2: ARIA FinOps Architecture Design

### 2.1 High-Level Architecture

```
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                    ARIA FINOPS PLATFORM                             │
│                  Cost Intelligence & Optimization                   │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
                                    │
                                    ▼
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                    INTERCEPTION LAYER                               │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                                     │
│  ┌──────────────┐  ┌──────────────┐  ┌──────────────┐             │
│  │ IDE Plugins  │  │ MCP Gateway  │  │ API Proxy    │             │
│  │ (VSCode,     │  │ (Agent       │  │ (OpenAI,     │             │
│  │  IntelliJ)   │  │  Requests)   │  │  Anthropic)  │             │
│  └──────┬───────┘  └──────┬───────┘  └──────┬───────┘             │
│         │                 │                 │                      │
│         └─────────────────┼─────────────────┘                      │
│                           ▼                                        │
│                  ┌─────────────────┐                               │
│                  │ Request Router  │                               │
│                  │ & Interceptor   │                               │
│                  └────────┬────────                               │
└───────────────────────────┼────────────────────────────────────────┘
                            │
                            ▼
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                    COST INTELLIGENCE ENGINE                         │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                                     │
│  ┌────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐        │
│  │  Real-Time Token Counter & Cost Calculator             │        │
│  ├────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤        │
│  │  • Prompt tokens (input)                               │        │
│  │  • Completion tokens (output)                          │        │
│  │  • Cached tokens (reduced cost)                        │        │
│  │  • Model-specific pricing lookup                       │        │
│  └────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘        │
│                                                                     │
│  ┌────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐        │
│  │  Context Enrichment Engine                             │        │
│  ├────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤        │
│  │  • User identity (from IDE session)                    │        │
│  │  • Agent ID (Copilot/Claude/MCP)                       │        │
│  │  • Model + version (GPT-4-turbo, Opus-3, etc.)         │        │
│  │  • IDE type (VSCode, IntelliJ, Cursor)                 │        │
